items.subtopic: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND AGE
items.title: Age-Related Incidence and Subtype Variability in ALL
items.content: ALL is most frequent in children, peaking at ages 3–4 years. In adults, incidence ranges from 0.7 to 1.8/100,000 per year, with variations across age groups. The Philadelphia chromosome-positive subtype (Ph+ ALL) occurs in half of elderly B-lineage patients.
keyPoints: ALL is the most frequent neoplastic disease in children, peaking at ages 3–4 years.
Incidence in adults ranges from 0.7 to 1.8/100,000 per year.
Higher incidence in adolescents and young adults (AYAs), decreasing in middle-aged adults but increasing again in the elderly.
Ph+ ALL is observed in half of elderly B-lineage patients.
Frequency of immunologic, cytogenetic, and genetic subtypes changes substantially with age.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND AGE | ETIOLOGY | CONGENITAL DISORDERS | INFECTIOUS AGENTS

items.subtopic: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | ETIOLOGY | CONGENITAL DISORDERS
items.title: Etiology and Congenital Risk Factors in ALL
items.content: The etiology of acute leukemias is largely unknown, though certain exposures increase risk. Patients with specific congenital disorders have a higher likelihood of developing ALL.
keyPoints: Etiology of acute leukemias is unknown; both internal and external factors influence incidence.
Exposure to ionizing radiation or chemicals, including prior chemotherapy, increases leukemia risk.
Secondary ALLs are observed after cytostatic treatment with alkylating agents and topoisomerase inhibitors.
Rare congenital chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., Klinefelter's syndrome, Fanconi's anemia) increase acute leukemia risk.
Patients with Down's syndrome have a 20-fold increased incidence of leukemia; ALL is more common in childhood, AML at older age.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND AGE | ETIOLOGY | CONGENITAL DISORDERS | INFECTIOUS AGENTS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD
items.title: Why Peripheral Blood Examination is Crucial in ALL Diagnosis
items.content: Peripheral blood examination, including WBC count and differential from a Wright-Giemsa-stained smear, is essential for diagnosing acute lymphoid leukemia (ALL). Despite the presence of leukemic blasts in bone marrow, peripheral blood may not always show these cells due to low counts or absence in 8% of patients.
keyPoints: WBC count is reduced or normal in ~40% of ALL patients.
Only 16% have a WBC above $100 \times 10^{5}/L$.
In 8% of cases, no circulating leukemic blast cells are observed in peripheral blood.
Peripheral blood often shows anemia, thrombocytopenia, and neutropenia.
Nearly one-third of patients have hemoglobin levels <7–8 g/dL.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD | BONE MARROW EXAMINATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE
items.title: When to Perform Lumbar Puncture in ALL Patients
items.content: Lumbar puncture is a critical diagnostic procedure for assessing CNS involvement in ALL. It should be performed when platelet counts are adequate (>20 × 10⁹/L) and without clinical hemorrhages, with intrathecal methotrexate administered to prevent CNS seeding of blast cells.
keyPoints: CNS leukemia is diagnosed if ≥5 cells/μL or leukemic blast cells are observed in cerebrospinal fluid.
Debate exists on timing: perform before treatment for early detection or after remission to avoid CNS seeding.
Lumbar puncture requires platelet count >20 × 10⁹/L and no clinical hemorrhages.
Intrathecal methotrexate is given at the first lumbar puncture to prevent CNS blast cell transfer.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD | BONE MARROW EXAMINATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES
items.title: L3 Morphology and Its Clinical Implications in Adult ALL
items.content: In the FAB classification, L3 morphology is significant as it indicates mature B-cell lineage ALL (B-ALL) in up to 5% of adult patients. This subtype has distinct clinical implications compared to L1 and L2 morphologies.
keyPoints: L3 morphology is indicative for mature B-cell lineage ALL (B-ALL).
Observed in up to 5% of adult patients.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES
items.title: Immunologic Classification and Its Therapeutic Implications in ALL
items.content: The immunologic classification of ALL involves using monoclonal antibodies to identify B-cell or T-cell markers. A marker is considered positive if >20% of cells are stained, aiding in subclassifying ALLs with therapeutic implications.
keyPoints: Monoclonal antibodies identify antigens on leukemic cells.
Marker positivity defined as >20% cell staining.
B-ALL constitutes more than 70% of adult cases.
T-ALL accounts for approximately 25% of adult ALLs.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE
items.title: Importance of Cytogenetic and Molecular Analysis in ALL
items.content: Cytogenetic and molecular analyses are crucial for defining subtypes of Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL), identifying prognostic markers, and determining targeted therapies. Techniques such as standard cytogenetics, fluorescence in situ hybridization, and reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction help detect specific genetic abnormalities like Ph+ ALL with t(9;22)(q34;q11) translocation.
keyPoints: Cytogenetic and molecular analyses are necessary for all ALL cases to define subtypes and prognostic markers.
Techniques include standard cytogenetics, fluorescence in situ hybridization, and reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction.
Detection of Ph+ ALL involves t(9;22)(q34;q11) translocation and BCR-ABL1 gene rearrangement.
Ph-like ALL is characterized by genetic lesions similar to Ph+ ALL but lacks BCR-ABL1. It occurs in 10% of children and 25–30% of young adults.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE
items.title: Role of Minimal Residual Disease (MRD) Detection in ALL Management
items.content: Minimal residual disease (MRD) detection is vital for identifying residual leukemic cells that are not visible under light microscopy. Techniques like flow cytometry, real-time quantitative PCR, and next-generation sequencing enhance sensitivity to detect MRD down to 0.1–0.001% or even 0.00001–0.000001 with advanced methods.
keyPoints: MRD detection identifies residual leukemic cells not visible under light microscopy.
Methods include flow cytometry, real-time quantitative PCR, and next-generation sequencing.
Detection sensitivity ranges from $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$, potentially increasing to $10^{-6}$ with advanced techniques.
Phenotypic aberrations are unique per patient and detectable in up to 95% of individuals.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES
items.title: Molecular Response as a Prognostic Factor in ALL
items.content: Achieving molecular complete response (CR) after induction therapy is crucial for improving disease-free and overall survival in both pediatric and adult patients with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (ALL). Patients who attain molecular CR have significantly better outcomes, with a disease-free survival rate of approximately 70%, compared to less than 40% for those who are MRD-positive.
keyPoints: Molecular complete response is the most relevant independent prognostic factor for ALL.
Disease-free survival rates: ~70% for molecular CR, <40% for MRD-positive patients.
Patients with molecular failure should consider targeted therapy and allogeneic SCT if possible.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES
items.title: Risk Stratification and Prognostic Factors in ALL
items.content: Prognostic factors at diagnosis, such as age, WBC count, immunophenotype, and cytogenetic/genetic aberrations, are used to stratify patients into risk groups. Standard-risk patients have no risk factors, while high-risk patients possess one or more. High-risk patients often proceed to SCT in the first complete remission.
keyPoints: Prognostic parameters include age, WBC count, immunophenotype, and cytogenetic/genetic aberrations.
Risk stratification divides patients into standard-risk (no risk factors) and high-risk (one or more risk factors).
High-risk patients are typically candidates for SCT in first complete remission.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES
items.title: Induction Therapy Goals and Regimens in ALL
items.content: The primary goal of induction therapy is to achieve a complete remission (CR) or molecular CR. Current regimens result in a CR rate of 80-90%, with higher rates for standard-risk patients (>90%) compared to high-risk patients (~60%). Induction therapy typically involves vincristine, glucocorticoids, anthracyclines, and may include cyclophosphamide or cytarabine. L-Asparaginase is specifically used in ALL treatment.
keyPoints: Induction therapy aims for CR or molecular CR.
CR rates: 80-90% overall; >90% for standard-risk, ~60% for high-risk patients.
Common drugs include vincristine, glucocorticoids, anthracyclines, cyclophosphamide, cytarabine, and L-Asparaginase.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF ALL PATIENTS ACCORDING TO AGE | PROPHYLAXIS AND TREATMENT OF CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LEUKEMIA
items.title: Age-Related Outcomes and Protocols in Acute Lymphoid Leukemia (ALL)
items.content: The prognosis of ALL varies significantly with age, showing high cure rates (~90%) in children but dropping to less than 10% in elderly or frail patients. Age-adapted treatment protocols are crucial due to differing hematologic and nonhematologic toxicities across age groups.
keyPoints: Cure rates ~90% in children with ALL
<10% cure rate in elderly or frail patients
Age-adapted protocols based on toxicity profiles
Major risk of relapse within the first 2 years, less likely after 5 years
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TREATMENT OF ALL PATIENTS ACCORDING TO AGE | PROPHYLAXIS AND TREATMENT OF CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LEUKEMIA

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF ALL PATIENTS ACCORDING TO AGE | PROPHYLAXIS AND TREATMENT OF CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LEUKEMIA
items.title: CNS Prophylaxis and Treatment Strategies in ALL
items.content: Prophylactic CNS therapy is crucial to prevent CNS leukemia. Options include intrathecal therapy, systemic high-dose chemotherapy, and cranial radiation therapy (CRT). Intrathecal therapy often involves methotrexate or a combination with cytosine arabinoside, while CRT typically uses 18–24 Gy over 16 days. Combined modalities reduce CNS relapse rates to 2–5%. Special considerations are needed for targeted therapies and immunotherapies due to varying blood-brain barrier penetration.
keyPoints: Intrathecal therapy often involves methotrexate or cytosine arabinoside
CRT typically uses 18-24 Gy over 16 days
CNS relapse rate reduced to 2–5% with combined modalities
Dasatinib and ponatinib cross the blood-brain barrier, unlike imatinib and nilotinib
Intrathecal therapy required for immunotherapies as antibodies generally do not enter CNS
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TREATMENT OF ALL PATIENTS ACCORDING TO AGE | PROPHYLAXIS AND TREATMENT OF CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LEUKEMIA

items.subtopic: STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION | PEDIATRIC-INSPIRED THERAPIES FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG ADULTS | ADULT ALL | ALL IN THE ELDERLY
items.title: Indications and Outcomes of Stem Cell Transplantation in Adult Acute Lymphoid Leukemia (ALL)
items.content: Stem cell transplantation (SCT) plays a crucial role in treating adult ALL, particularly for high-risk patients. Peripheral blood cells are increasingly used over bone marrow. SCT is recommended for high-risk patients defined by conventional prognostic factors or MRD positivity, with survival rates of 50% or greater when transplanted during the first complete remission (CR). The reduction in transplant-related mortality from 20–30% to 10–15% has improved outcomes significantly. For standard-risk patients with sustained molecular remission, allogeneic SCT is not recommended in the first CR. Autologous SCT should be limited to MRD-negative and BCR-ABL-negative patients due to its higher relapse rate.
keyPoints: Peripheral blood cells are increasingly used as a stem cell source over bone marrow.
SCT is recommended for high-risk adult ALL patients, defined by conventional prognostic factors or MRD positivity.
High-risk patients transplanted in first CR have a survival rate of 50% or greater.
Transplant-related mortality has decreased from 20–30% to 10–15%, improving outcomes.
Allogeneic SCT is not recommended for standard-risk patients with sustained molecular remission in the first CR.
Autologous SCT should be restricted to MRD-negative and BCR-ABL-negative patients due to higher relapse rates.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION | PEDIATRIC-INSPIRED THERAPIES FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG ADULTS | ADULT ALL | ALL IN THE ELDERLY

items.subtopic: STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION | PEDIATRIC-INSPIRED THERAPIES FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG ADULTS | ADULT ALL | ALL IN THE ELDERLY
items.title: Pediatric-Inspired Therapies for Adolescents and Young Adults (AYAs) with ALL
items.content: Pediatric-inspired therapies for AYAs focus on higher doses of specific drugs like glucocorticoids, vincristine, and L-asparaginase, while reducing the use of myeloablative anthracyclines or alkylating agents. This approach emphasizes strict adherence to time-dose intensity, minimizing the role of SCT. These therapies have resulted in overall survival rates for AYAs ranging from 70–80%.
keyPoints: Higher doses and more applications of specific drugs like glucocorticoids, vincristine, and L-asparaginase are used.
Fewer myeloablative anthracyclines or alkylating agents are employed.
Strict adherence to time-dose intensity is crucial.
The role of SCT is minimized in this approach.
Overall survival rates for AYAs are 70–80%.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION | PEDIATRIC-INSPIRED THERAPIES FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG ADULTS | ADULT ALL | ALL IN THE ELDERLY

items.subtopic: TARGETED THERAPIES | TYROSINE KINASE INHIBITORS IN PHILADELPHIA-POSITIVE ALL
items.title: Impact of TKIs on Philadelphia-positive Acute Lymphoid Leukemia (Ph+ ALL) Outcomes
items.content: The introduction of tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) has significantly improved outcomes in patients with Ph+ ALL. Initially, the use of imatinib, a first-generation TKI, increased complete remission (CR) rates from 60-70% to 80-90%, and overall survival over 5 to 10 years improved to 50-70%. Second-generation TKIs like dasatinib and nilotinib offer faster and deeper molecular responses, translating into a survival benefit. Third-generation TKI ponatinib is effective against mutations conferring resistance to earlier generations.
keyPoints: Ph+ ALL constitutes ~25% of adult B-ALL patients, increasing to ~50% among the elderly.
Pre-imatinib CR rates were 60–70%, with survival at ~10%. With imatinib, CR increased to 80–90%, and overall survival improved to 50–70% over 5-10 years.
Second-generation TKIs (dasatinib, nilotinib) achieve faster and deeper molecular responses.
Third-generation TKI ponatinib is effective against T315I mutation resistance.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TARGETED THERAPIES | TYROSINE KINASE INHIBITORS IN PHILADELPHIA-POSITIVE ALL | IMMUNOTHERAPEUTIC APPROACHES

items.subtopic: TARGETED THERAPIES | IMMUNOTHERAPEUTIC APPROACHES
items.title: Role of Immunotherapeutic Approaches in Acute Lymphoid Leukemia (ALL)
items.content: Immunotherapy, particularly monoclonal antibodies targeting specific antigens like CD19, CD20, and CD22, is transforming ALL treatment. Rituximab, an anti-CD20 antibody, has notably improved outcomes in de novo Burkitt's leukemia/lymphoma when combined with intensive chemotherapy, increasing overall survival to >80%. This approach is now standard in most B-ALL regimens.
keyPoints: Monoclonal antibodies target antigens such as CD19, CD20, and CD22 on B-lineage blast cells.
Rituximab (anti-CD20) improves outcomes in de novo Burkitt's leukemia/lymphoma with survival >80% when combined with chemotherapy.
Rituximab is included at a dose of 375 mg/m^2 on day -1 before chemotherapy in most B-ALL regimens.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TARGETED THERAPIES | TYROSINE KINASE INHIBITORS IN PHILADELPHIA-POSITIVE ALL | IMMUNOTHERAPEUTIC APPROACHES

items.question: Which generation of TKIs is effective in tumors bearing the T315I mutation?
items.explanation: The third-generation TKI ponatinib is effective in tumors with mutations like T315I that confer resistance to earlier-generation TKIs.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TARGETED THERAPIES | TYROSINE KINASE INHIBITORS IN PHILADELPHIA-POSITIVE ALL | IMMUNOTHERAPEUTIC APPROACHES

items.subtopic: TARGETED THERAPIES | TYROSINE KINASE INHIBITORS IN PHILADELPHIA-POSITIVE ALL | IMMUNOTHERAPEUTIC APPROACHES
items.question: Which monoclonal antibody is used in the treatment of de novo Burkitt's leukemia/lymphoma and has improved patient outcomes?
items.explanation: The anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab has improved the outcome of patients with de novo Burkitt's leukemia/lymphoma.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TARGETED THERAPIES | TYROSINE KINASE INHIBITORS IN PHILADELPHIA-POSITIVE ALL | IMMUNOTHERAPEUTIC APPROACHES

items.subtopic: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | INFECTIOUS AGENTS
items.statement: Viral infections are the primary cause of acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL).
items.explanation: No direct evidence implicates viruses as a major cause of human acute leukemia.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND AGE | ETIOLOGY | CONGENITAL DISORDERS | INFECTIOUS AGENTS

items.subtopic: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Exposure to ionizing radiation is associated with an increased risk of developing ALL.
items.explanation: Ionizing radiation exposure is linked to an increased risk of leukemia.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · Acute Lymphoid Leukemia | INCIDENCE AND AGE | ETIOLOGY | CONGENITAL DISORDERS | INFECTIOUS AGENTS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD | BONE MARROW EXAMINATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE
items.statement: Bone marrow examination is not necessary to distinguish between AML and ALL.
items.explanation: A bone marrow biopsy is essential to confirm the diagnosis of acute leukemia and to distinguish between AML and ALL.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD | BONE MARROW EXAMINATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD | BONE MARROW EXAMINATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE
items.statement: In approximately 8% of ALL patients, circulating leukemic blast cells are not observed in the peripheral blood.
items.explanation: It is noteworthy that in 8% of ALL patients, no circulating leukemic blast cells were observed.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · DIAGNOSIS AND CLASSIFICATION | PERIPHERAL BLOOD | BONE MARROW EXAMINATION | LUMBAR PUNCTURE

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES
items.statement: L1 and L2 morphologies in ALL have significant clinical consequences.
items.explanation: L1 and L2 morphology has no clinical consequences.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES
items.statement: Biphenotypic leukemias express markers of both lymphoid and myeloid lineages on the same leukemic cells.
items.explanation: Biphenotypic leukemias are defined as those expressing markers of both lymphoid and myeloid lineages on the same leukemic cells.

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE
items.statement: Ph-like ALL increases in frequency with age, similar to Ph+ ALL.
items.explanation: Unlike Ph+ ALL, the frequency of Ph-like ALL does not increase further with age.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE
items.statement: The detection limit for MRD using flow cytometry is typically $10^{-3}-10^{-5}$.
items.explanation: Methods like flow cytometry can detect MRD with a sensitivity of $10^{-3}-10^{-5}$.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.statement: 20–30% of adult ALL patients who are MRD negative after induction will relapse.
items.explanation: Despite being MRD-negative after induction, 20-30% of adult ALL patients may still experience a relapse.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: TREATMENT PRINCIPLES
items.statement: The duration of maintenance therapy for T-ALL and B-ALL is typically less than one year.
items.explanation: The duration of maintenance therapy for T-ALL and B-ALL is 2–2.5 years, except for Burkitt's leukemia.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES

items.statement: CNS relapse in ALL is usually accompanied by bone marrow involvement.
items.explanation: Relapse in CNS is usually accompanied by bone marrow involvement.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · TREATMENT OF ALL PATIENTS ACCORDING TO AGE | PROPHYLAXIS AND TREATMENT OF CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LEUKEMIA

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF ALL PATIENTS ACCORDING TO AGE | PROPHYLAXIS AND TREATMENT OF CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM LEUKEMIA
items.statement: Patients with initial CNS involvement have a significantly lower overall survival compared to CNS-negative patients.
items.explanation: Patients with initial CNS involvement have similar overall survival as CNS-negative patients.

items.subtopic: STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION | PEDIATRIC-INSPIRED THERAPIES FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG ADULTS | ADULT ALL | ALL IN THE ELDERLY
items.statement: For standard-risk adult ALL patients with sustained molecular remission, allogeneic SCT in first CR is recommended.
items.explanation: Allogeneic SCT in first CR is not recommended for standard-risk patients with sustained molecular remission.

items.statement: Intensive chemotherapy has improved CR rates in elderly patients to ~90%.
items.explanation: Specific elderly ALL protocols with less intensive therapy have improved CR rates to ~90%, not intensive chemotherapy.

items.statement: Rituximab is primarily used in the treatment of Philadelphia-negative acute lymphoblastic leukemia.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that rituximab, an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody, improves outcomes for patients with de novo Burkitt's leukemia/lymphoma and is included in most B-ALL regimens.

items.assertion: A lumbar puncture should be delayed until remission is achieved to avoid CNS seeding.
items.reason: Performing a lumbar puncture before treatment starts allows for immediate CNS-specific therapy if CNS disease is detected.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why a lumbar puncture should be delayed.

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES
items.assertion: Pre-B-ALL is characterized by the expression of cytoplasmic immunoglobulin.
items.reason: Common ALL is also characterized by the presence of cytoplasmic immunoglobulin.
items.explanation: Pre-B-ALL is characterized by the expression of cytoplasmic immunoglobulin, which is negative in common ALL.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MORPHOLOGIC SUBTYPES IN ALL | IMMUNOLOGIC SUBTYPES

items.assertion: Collection of bone marrow at diagnosis is essential for follow-up of MRD.
items.reason: Phenotypic aberrations are unique to each patient with ALL and can be detected in up to 95% of individuals.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why collecting bone marrow at diagnosis is crucial, as it allows for the identification of individual markers needed for MRD follow-up.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · CYTOGENETIC AND MOLECULAR ANALYSIS | MINIMAL RESIDUAL DISEASE

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES
items.assertion: Patients with molecular complete remission after induction therapy have a significantly superior outcome.
items.reason: These patients have a disease-free survival rate of ~70% compared to <40% for MRD-positive patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with molecular complete remission have superior outcomes.

items.subtopic: TREATMENT PRINCIPLES
items.assertion: Dexamethasone is often preferred to prednisone in ALL treatment.
items.reason: It penetrates the blood-brain barrier and acts on resting leukemic blast cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why dexamethasone is often preferred to prednisone.
items.reference: Acute Lymphoid Leukemia · MOLECULAR RESPONSE AFTER INDUCTION THERAPY AND IMPACT ON OUTCOME | PROGNOSTIC FACTORS, RISK STRATIFICATION, AND MRD | TREATMENT PRINCIPLES

items.subtopic: STEM CELL TRANSPLANTATION | PEDIATRIC-INSPIRED THERAPIES FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG ADULTS | ADULT ALL | ALL IN THE ELDERLY
items.assertion: For all relapsed adult ALL patients, an allogeneic SCT is the only curative option.
items.reason: Allogeneic SCT provides a survival rate of 50% or greater for high-risk patients transplanted in first CR.
items.explanation: Both statements are true; however, the reason does not explain why allogeneic SCT is the only curative option for relapsed adult ALL.
